title: Welding Tips: Heat Control, Penetration, and Diagnosis
summary: This section covers how to evaluate weld quality through visual inspection and a strike test, how to clean a completed weld, and how to diagnose and correct common wire weld problems related to heat, penetration, voltage, wire feed speed, travel speed, and CTWD.
key_facts: Examine a weld after it has cooled and slag has been removed to evaluate technique.
TIG welding requires experience and skill beyond the scope of this manual.
A good weld will deform but not break when struck with a heavy hammer.
A poor weld will be brittle and snap at the weld when struck.
The strike test WILL damage the weld and is only an indicator of technique.
Use a chipping hammer to remove slag from flux-cored or stick welds.
Use a wire brush or angle grinder to further clean or shape the weld.
Increasing weld current or decreasing travel speed increases workpiece heat and penetration.
Decreasing weld current or increasing travel speed reduces workpiece heat and penetration.
Voltage too low or wire feed too slow results in a weld requiring increased output voltage or wire feed speed.
CTWD too long or wrong polarity requires checking polarity and maintaining less than 1/2 inch CTWD.
Travel speed too slow requires faster travel; travel speed too fast requires slower travel.

steps: 1. After two scraps have been welded together and the weld has cooled, clamp one scrap in a sturdy vise.
2. Stay clear from underneath while you strike the opposite scrap with a heavy hammer, preferably a dead-blow hammer.
3. A GOOD WELD will deform but not break; a POOR WELD will be brittle and snap at the weld.
1. A weld from flux-cored wire welding or stick welding will be covered by slag. Use a chipping hammer to knock this off. Be careful not to damage the weld or base material.
2. Use a wire brush to further clean the weld or use an angle grinder (sold separately) to shape the weld.

warnings: WEAR ANSI-APPROVED SAFETY GOGGLES DURING THIS PROCEDURE.
CAUTION! This test WILL damage the weld it is performed on. This test is ONLY an indicator of weld technique and is not intended to test working welds.
TO PREVENT SERIOUS INJURY: Continue to wear ANSI-approved Welding safety goggles and protective wear when cleaning a weld. Sparks or chips may fly when cleaning.
NOTICE: TIG welding is a complicated process, requiring experience and skill to achieve successful results. Training beyond the scope of this manual is required to TIG weld properly.
